actions.label: Hire a Chief Operating Officer
actions.description: Hand the machine to a COO. They run delivery, systems, and the team — and each month they automatically execute the operations move that most reduces your dependence on you. The business stops breaking when you step away.
actions.lesson: Key-person dependency is the silent killer of business value — a company that needs you isn't an asset, it's a job. A COO turns your operation into a system that runs (and sells) without you.
actions.narrative_success: Your COO has the wheel. Operations now runs itself — each month they execute the smartest systems-and-delivery move so the business holds together without you. Their pay scales with the company (a market salary plus performance bonuses).
actions.narrative_failure: The COO is onboard but still learning the operation. They'll be driving your ops moves from here. Their pay scales with the company.

actions.label: Do the Work Yourself
actions.description: You are the business. Every client, every deliverable, every email. It's unsustainable, but it gets the work delivered and the invoices paid.
actions.lesson: Do it yourself first — not forever. You can't systematize or delegate what you don't deeply understand. Master the work, bank the cash, then escape it before you become the bottleneck.
actions.narrative_success: Everything got done. Everything. You're exhausted, but every client is happy — because you did it all personally, and the invoices got paid.
actions.narrative_failure: You dropped a ball. One client got a late delivery. Nothing catastrophic, but a reminder that one person can't do everything.

actions.label: Hire Your First Contractor
actions.description: Offload delivery work to someone else for the first time. Terrifying. Necessary. It buys you capacity.
actions.lesson: Your first hire won't be as good as you — and that's fine. 80% done by someone else beats 100% done by you alone. Trading money for delivery capacity is the whole game.
actions.narrative_success: She's not as good as you. She's 80% as good, and that 80% just bought you back twenty hours a week of capacity.
actions.narrative_failure: The contractor needs more hand-holding than expected. You're spending time managing instead of doing — but that's the transition.

actions.label: Build a Delivery Team
actions.description: Hire two to three people dedicated to fulfillment. You manage them. They do the work. Capacity scales.
actions.lesson: A dedicated delivery team is the leap from freelancer to business. The work happens whether you're in the room or not — that's a job becoming an asset with real capacity.
actions.narrative_success: The team handled this month's workload without a single escalation to you. You built something that works without you.
actions.narrative_failure: The team is hired and training. Quality isn't where you want it yet. You're managing more than doing — the awkward middle phase.

actions.label: Build Fulfillment System
actions.description: Systematize delivery end-to-end: intake, production, QA, delivery, follow-up. All documented, all tracked — so you can take on far more without quality cracking.
actions.lesson: Turn delivery into an assembly line — intake, production, QA, delivery, follow-up, all documented. Consistency at scale lets you grow capacity without your quality cracking.
actions.narrative_success: A new client moves through your system like a product on an assembly line — consistent, reliable, scalable. This is a real business now.
actions.narrative_failure: The system is built for 80% of cases. The other 20% still need you. But that's 80% fewer fires.

actions.label: Build Proprietary IP
actions.description: Develop proprietary tools, methodology, or technology that competitors can't replicate. Your moat — and your pricing power.
actions.lesson: A methodology competitors can't copy is a moat. Proprietary IP gives you pricing power and capacity to capture more value — not a commodity competing on price.
actions.narrative_success: The proprietary framework is built and branded. Clients now come to you for the methodology, not just the service.
actions.narrative_failure: The IP is developed but not yet differentiated enough. More iteration needed. Still, the foundation is laid.

actions.label: Vertical Integration
actions.description: Bring a supplier or distribution channel in-house. Control more of the value chain, keep more margin on every dollar you deliver.
actions.lesson: Own more of the value chain, keep more of every dollar. Controlling your supply or distribution turns a cost center into a margin advantage competitors can't match.
actions.narrative_success: You own the supply chain now. Margins jumped. Competitors are paying retail for what you produce in-house.
actions.narrative_failure: The integration is messier than expected. You're saving on COGS but spending more on management overhead.

actions.label: Multi-Market Expansion
actions.description: Open a second location or market. Clone what works into a new geography or vertical to multiply your capacity.
actions.lesson: Once the playbook works, clone it. A proven system dropped into a new market or vertical multiplies the business — and its delivery capacity — without reinventing it.
actions.narrative_success: Location two is open and profitable in month three. The playbook translated. You're an empire now.
actions.narrative_failure: The new market is slower than expected. Overhead is real. It'll take six months to break even — if it does.

actions.label: Document Your First SOP
actions.description: Write down how you actually do the work. Step by step. So someone else could follow it without calling you.
actions.lesson: Document before you delegate. An SOP moves the process out of your head and onto paper — the first real step from operator to owner.
actions.narrative_success: It took twelve hours to write what you do in your sleep. But now it exists outside your head for the first time.
actions.narrative_failure: The SOP is written but it's incomplete. You realize how much of your process is instinct, not system.

actions.label: Automate Repetitive Tasks
actions.description: Set up email templates, invoice automation, scheduling tools. Reclaim hours from admin busywork.
actions.lesson: Automate the repeatable so nothing slips through the cracks. Software doesn't forget to follow up — it protects the client experience while handing you back your hours.
actions.narrative_success: Invoices go out automatically now. Scheduling is self-serve. You just got back five hours a week you didn't know you were losing.
actions.narrative_failure: The automation is partially working. Invoices are automated; scheduling still needs manual intervention.

actions.label: Implement Project Management
actions.description: Deploy real project tracking — Asana, Monday, or similar. Everyone sees what's due, who owns it, and what's late.
actions.lesson: Visibility is control. When everyone sees what's due, who owns it, and what's late, things stop falling through the cracks — and you stop being the human task-tracker.
actions.narrative_success: For the first time, you can see every active project, every deadline, every bottleneck — in one screen. This changes everything.
actions.narrative_failure: The tool is set up. Adoption is mixed. Your best contractor refuses to update her tasks. People problems, not software problems.

actions.label: Build a Hiring Pipeline
actions.description: Stop panic-hiring. Build a repeatable process for finding, vetting, and onboarding new team members.
actions.lesson: Recruit before you're desperate. A repeatable pipeline means you hire for fit, not just to stop the bleeding — and the cost of a bad panic-hire dwarfs the cost of being patient.
actions.narrative_success: You have a job post template, an interview script, and a 30-day onboarding plan. Next hire takes days to onboard, not weeks.
actions.narrative_failure: The pipeline exists on paper. In practice, you're still gut-checking candidates over coffee. But the structure is started.

actions.label: Hire a Manager
actions.description: Promote or hire someone to manage the team so you manage one person instead of five.
actions.lesson: Manage one person instead of five. A management layer buys back your time and lets the team grow past your personal bandwidth — you stop being the ceiling.
actions.narrative_success: She ran the Monday meeting without you this week. The team didn't even notice you weren't there. That's the goal.
actions.narrative_failure: The new manager is learning. Team dynamics shifted. Two people aren't happy about the new layer. Growing pains.

actions.label: Full Systemization
actions.description: The business runs without you. SOPs for everything. Manager handles daily ops. You're the owner, not the operator.
actions.lesson: When the business runs without you, you've won the real prize: not just income, but freedom. You become the owner, not the operator — and your time becomes yours again.
actions.narrative_success: You took a week off. Revenue was the same when you came back. This is what freedom looks like.
actions.narrative_failure: Almost. Two client issues escalated to you during your 'vacation.' Close, but the safety net has holes.

actions.label: Hire Fractional CFO
actions.description: A part-time CFO who manages your finances strategically — cash flow, debt structure, banking relationships, tax planning. They do what you've been winging.
actions.lesson: Delegate the money function to someone who does it strategically — not on the side at midnight. Financial leadership is a force multiplier: better debt structure, lower taxes, smarter cash flow.
actions.narrative_success: Your CFO restructured two loan terms in her first month, found $3,000 in unnecessary expenses, and projected your cash flow for the next six months. You should have done this two years ago.
actions.narrative_failure: Onboarding is taking longer than expected — your books were messier than she anticipated. But she's cleaning them up.

actions.label: Hire HR Manager
actions.description: Someone who handles hiring, onboarding, culture, and compliance. Your team grows without you interviewing every candidate.
actions.lesson: Past a handful of people, hiring and culture become a job of their own. An HR lead lets the team grow without you interviewing every candidate or refereeing every issue.
actions.narrative_success: First hire she made on her own was better than your last three. The job posting, screening, and onboarding all happened while you were focused on strategy.
actions.narrative_failure: She's building processes. The org chart is getting formalized. It feels bureaucratic — but your team of 6+ needs it.

actions.label: Build Quality Control
actions.description: Create a checklist and review process so work goes out right the first time, even when you're not looking. Fewer mistakes, fewer clients lost.
actions.lesson: Quality has to survive your absence. A simple checklist keeps the work right when you're not the one doing it — and consistent quality is what keeps clients from leaving.
actions.narrative_success: Zero client complaints this month. The checklist caught two errors before they went out the door — and nobody churned.
actions.narrative_failure: The checklist exists. People are using it... sometimes. Consistency will take another month of enforcement.

actions.label: Build Client Onboarding
actions.description: Create a smooth intake process — contracts, expectations, kickoff. Nail the first week and clients stay longer.
actions.lesson: A great client experience is designed, not improvised. Nail the first week and you cut churn, earn referrals, and justify higher prices.
actions.narrative_success: New clients now get a welcome packet, a signed scope, and a kickoff call. You look like a real company now — and they stick around.
actions.narrative_failure: The onboarding flow is built but clunky. Half the clients still have questions the packet should have answered.

actions.label: Hire Client Success Manager
actions.description: Someone who owns the client relationship after the sale. Retention goes up, churn goes down, and you stop being the only person clients call.
actions.lesson: Keeping a client is cheaper than winning one. Delighted clients don't just stay — they hand you testimonials and referrals, the cheapest, highest-converting growth there is. Retention IS marketing.
actions.narrative_success: Three clients sent thank-you emails this month — none of them were to you. Your CSM is building relationships you used to own alone.
actions.narrative_failure: The CSM is learning your clients. Some awkward handoffs, but the clients appreciate having a dedicated contact.
